coredata.dc:title: Enhanced charge extraction enabled by amide-functionalized carbon quantum dots modifier for efficient carbon-based perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ziyu
He, Benlin
Wei, Meng
Liu, Weilin
Li, Xueke
Zhu, Jingwei
Chen, Haiyan
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: The effective improvement of interfacial charge extraction and inhibition of charge recombination are crucial for promoting the performance of perovskite (PVK) solar cells (PSCs). Here, the N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide-functionalized carbon quantum dots (MBA/CQDs) with defects passivation effect and appropriate energy levels are proposed as a back interface modifier for carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs. The functionalization of MBA molecules not only increases the content of sp 2 hybrid carbon in CQDs, but also expands the passivation effect of CQDs on surface defects of PVK films by introducing carbonyl and amino groups, which improves the carrier transport and reduces the carrier nonradiative recombination. Additionally, the modification of MBA/CQDs provides an intermediate energy level at the PVK/carbon back interface, which boosts hole extraction and lowers energy loss. Finally, a leading-edge power conversion efficiency of 10.40 % is derived for the MBA/CQDs modified champion device, which is significantly improved compared to the 7.02 % efficiency for the original CsPbBr3 PSCs. Furthermore, the unpackaged CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs have an excellent stability, maintaining an initial efficiency of 92 % after 30 days of storage at high humidity (85 % relative humidity) and temperature (85 °C) in air.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based perovskite solar cells
Interface modification
Amide-functionalized carbon quantum dots
Defects passivation
Charge extraction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021ME037), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22179051 and 61604143 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFE0111000), Special Fund of Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqnz20221141), and Foundation of Key Laboratory of Advanced Technique & Preparation for Renewable Energy Materials, Ministry of Education, Yunnan Normal University (OF2022-02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of MBA functionalized CQDs. (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM image of MBA/CQDs. (d) FTIR spectra of CQDs, MBA and MBA/CQDs. (e) Raman and (f) PL spectra of CQDs and MBA/CQDs with different mass ratios. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) Cs 3d, (h) Pb 4f and (i) Br 3d of CsPbBr3 films without and with MBA/CQDs.

(a) SEM images of the pristine, CQDs, 8 MBA/CQDs, 6 MBA/CQDs and 4 MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 films (from left to right). (b) EDS mapping images of C, N, O, Cs, Pb and Br elements of MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 film. (c) XRD patterns of various CsPbBr3 films. (d) Dark J-V curves for the hole-only devices with a structure of FTO/NiO/CsPbBr3/without and with different CQDs/Carbon. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of various CsPbBr3 films.

(a) Schematic illustration and (b) cross-sectional SEM image of carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs. (c) Energy level diagram of the charge transfer process in carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs. (d) J 1/2-V curves of FTO/NiOx/CsPbBr3/with and without different CQDs/Carbon. (e) TRPL and (f) steady-state PL spectra of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/with and without different CQDs. (g) The characteristic J-V curves, (h) IPCE spectra and (i) Steady-state power output curves of carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with and without different CQDs.

(a) Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots, (b) V OC and (c) J SC dependence on light intensity, (d) electron lifetime, (e) dark J-V curves and (f) Nyquist plots and fitting lines of various carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs.

(a) Normalized PCE, V OC, J SC and FF of the original and MBA/CQDs tailored devices without encapsulation as a function of time in the ambient with 85 % RH at 85 °C. (b) Water contact angle of various PVK films.
The key photovoltaic parameters of carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with and without different CQDs.

head.ce:title: Enhanced charge extraction enabled by amide-functionalized carbon quantum dots modifier for efficient carbon-based perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Carbon Neutrality, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Carbon Neutrality, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The MBA/CQDs with low edge defects are used as an interface modifier to holistically passivate the surface defects of PVK film and regulate the interfacial energetics, which helps the carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs achieve a champion PCE up to 10.40% and excellent humidity-heat stability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective improvement of interfacial charge extraction and inhibition of charge recombination are crucial for promoting the performance of perovskite (PVK) solar cells (PSCs). Here, the N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide-functionalized carbon quantum dots (MBA/CQDs) with defects passivation effect and appropriate energy levels are proposed as a back interface modifier for carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs. The functionalization of MBA molecules not only increases the content of sp 2 hybrid carbon in CQDs, but also expands the passivation effect of CQDs on surface defects of PVK films by introducing carbonyl and amino groups, which improves the carrier transport and reduces the carrier nonradiative recombination. Additionally, the modification of MBA/CQDs provides an intermediate energy level at the PVK/carbon back interface, which boosts hole extraction and lowers energy loss. Finally, a leading-edge power conversion efficiency of 10.40 % is derived for the MBA/CQDs modified champion device, which is significantly improved compared to the 7.02 % efficiency for the original CsPbBr3 PSCs. Furthermore, the unpackaged CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs have an excellent stability, maintaining an initial efficiency of 92 % after 30 days of storage at high humidity (85 % relative humidity) and temperature (85 °C) in air.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The organic–inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted growing attention in the global photovoltaic field due to their several benefits including a broad spectral response range, high light absorption coefficient, long carrier lifetime and diffusion length, and their power conversion efficiency (PCE) has been increased to 26.1 % in the short span of ten years [1–4]. However, the commercialization of the organic–inorganic hybrid PSCs is significantly constrained because of the poor stability of the organic–inorganic hybrid perovskites (PVK) photosensitizer, in which the organic component is easily volatile at high temperature and inorganic lead iodide is precipitated under the attack of moisture [5–7]. Thankfully, Cs ions can be used to form all-inorganic CsPbIxBr3-x (0 ≤ x ≤ 3) PVKs in place of the organic ions to overcome the thermal instability of the organic–inorganic hybrid PVKs [8–10]. The CsPbBr3 has emerged as the most expected light-absorbing layer among all-inorganic PVKs due to its excellent tolerance to dampness and heat and fast carrier diffusion [11,12]. But the PCE of the classic carbon-based all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs is still far from reaching its theoretical limit efficiency of 16.5 % [13], which is mainly owing to the poor charge extraction induced by the energy level mismatch at the CsPbBr3/carbon back interface and the low hole selectivity of carbon electrode as well as the recombination of charge resulted from defect states on the surface of CsPbBr3 films [14–16].

As previously reported, interface engineering is an effective strategy to adjust the interfacial energetics, passivate surface defects of PVK film, boost charge extraction and prevent moisture intrusion to improving the PCE and long-term stability of the PSCs [17–20]. Many materials, such as inorganic or organic substances (e.g., VO2, 2-mercapto-1-methylimidazole, amino-functionalized polymer) [21–23], quantum dots (QDs) (e.g., PbS QDs, WS2 QDs) [24,25] and carbon materials (e.g., graphene, carbon QDs (CQDs), carbon nanotubes, graphene oxides) [26–29], have been widely employed as advanced interface modifiers. Among them, CQDs with a tunable energy level structure and sp 2 hybrid carbon show excellent function in improving band alignment and charge extraction [30–32]. Moreover, the rich oxygen-containing groups on CQDs surface can serve as Lewis bases to heal the cation defects of PVK film [30]. However, these functional groups also result in an increase in edge defects of CQDs, which act as the centers for non-radiative recombination to reduce charge transfer and extraction [33]. Although this problem can be overcome by treatment with reducing agents such as hydrazines or metals [34,35], the inherent passivation ability of CQDs will inevitably be diminished. Therefore, it is very worthwhile to take reasonable tactics to reduce the defects of CQDs while maximizing their passivation effect and charge extraction ability to further improve the performance of the modified PSCs.

In this study, a N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide-functionalized CQDs (MBA/CQDs) modifier is designed and inserted into the interface between PVK and carbon electrode to build high-efficiency carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs. The treatment of CQDs with MBA induce a decrease in edge defects by reducing the oxygen content via a dehydration condensation reaction between the carboxyl on CQDs surface and amino in MBA, which increases the content of sp 2 hybrid carbon in CQDs to improve the carrier transport behavior. Meanwhile, the carbonyl and amino introduced by MBA further expand the passivation of CQDs toward the defects on PVK film surface. Moreover, the introduction of MBA/CQDs optimizes the energy level matching at the PVK/Carbon interface. The carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs finally achieve a remarkably increased PCE of 10.40 % with an open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.613 V due to the improved charge extraction and the reduced charge recombination and energy loss, which is an exciting improvement compared to the original device’s PCE of 7.02 % with a V OC of 1.431 V. Importantly, the water plugging characteristic of MBA enables a higher hydrophobicity of the PVK film modified with MBA/CQDs, coupled with the defects passivation of CsPbBr3 by MBA/CQDs, which significantly improves the environmental stability of the device. The unencapsulated carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs modified with MBA/CQDs maintain a 92 % of initial efficiency after aging in an air atmosphere of 85 % relative humidity (RH) and 85 °C for 30 days.
The detailed experimental information, including raw materials, preparation of MBA/CQDs and CsPbBr3 PSCs, measurements and characterizations, has been indicated in the supplementary materials.

The treatment of CQDs with MBA can occur a dehydration condensation reaction between the secondary amine group in the MBA molecule and the carboxyl group on the surface of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1
a, which reduces the oxygen element of CQDs to decrease the defects and immobilizes the MBA on CQDs to improve the passivation effect. As shown in Fig. 1 b, the CQDs with a size of about 5 nm are attached by the MBA molecules and uniformly dispersed. The lattice spacing in the HRTEM image of MBA/CQDs (Fig. 1 c) is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the surface spacing of the (1–100) plane of graphene, which suggests a high sp 2 hybrid carbon bonding in this CQDs [36]. Additionally, the isopropanol solutions of CQDs and MBA/CQDs are uniform without precipitate, as seen in Fig. S1, which is beneficial to evenly spreading and forming a film. Under ultraviolet irradiation, the isopropanol solutions of CQDs and MBA/CQDs exhibit a blue fluorescence compared with the isopropanol solution of MBA, further confirming the successful preparation of CQDs.

To verify the dehydration condensation reaction between MBA and CQDs, the FTIR spectra were characterized and shown in Fig. 1 d. Compared with pure CQDs, the stretching vibration peak of –CO-N- appears at 1367 cm−1 and the stretching vibration peak of –COOH disappears at 1409 cm−1 in MBA/CQDs. At the same time, the –NH vibration peak intensity of MBA/CQDs at 3282 cm−1 is obviously weakened compared with that of MBA. The above phenomena corroborate the occurrence of a dehydration condensation reaction between MBA and CQDs. Notably, the vibration peak of the –NH still appears in MBA/CQDs because of the existences of the unreacted amino groups in the MBA molecule and the residues of the NH4HCO3 decomposition during the synthesis of CQDs (as demonstrated by the presence of –NH vibration peak in the FTIR spectrum of CQDs), which will play a passivation effect on the ionic defect states of the PVK. The Raman spectra of CQDs and various MBA/CQDs were characterized to investigate the passivation effect of MBA molecules on CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 e. The intensity ratio of D to G bands (I D/I G) for MBA/CQDs, especially for 6 MBA/CQDs, is significantly reduced compared with that for the CQDs, indicating a lower edge defects and a higher sp 2 hybrid carbon bonding for the MBA/CQDs [37]. The higher PL peak intensity of MBA/CQDs with a blue shift than that of CQDs in Fig. 1 f also indicates a decreased defect for the former samples, which cross-validates the results of Raman spectra. The dehydration condensation reaction between MBA and CQDs induced the reduction of oxygen content for CQDs is responsible for the decreased edge defects, which helps to maximize charge transport and extraction upon using MBA/CQDs as an interface modifier.

To probe the interaction between MBA/CQDs and PVK, the XPS spectra of PVK films with and without MBA/CQDs modification were tested, as shown in Fig. 1 g-i. The Cs 3d and Pb 4f characteristic peaks of the MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 film shift towards low binding energy as compared to that of the pristine one [38], demonstrating that the electron cloud density of Cs and Pb atoms increases while their electron affinity decreases due to the formation of coordination bonds between C=O, C-N, N-H in MBA/CQDs and Pb2+, Cs+ of CsPbBr3 film, as demonstrated by the FTIR spectra results shown in Fig. S2 . In addition, the shift of the resonance signal peak of the N-H for the MBA/CQDs + CsPbBr3 precursor sample to the low-field direction (△δ = 0.4 ppm) compared with that for the MBA/CQDs (Fig. S3) indicates a hydrogen bonding interaction between the N-H and the halogen Br of CsPbBr3 [39], which is confirmed by the positive shift of Br 3d for the MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 film (Fig. 1 i). All the above results illustrate that the functional groups of MBA/CQDs can bond with the uncoordinated Pb2+, Cs+ and Br− of CsPbBr3 film, which holistically passivates the ion defect states on PVK film surface to significantly suppress carrier recombination in the device.

Fig. 2
a shows the top-view SEM images of CsPbBr3 PVK films modified without and with different QDs. The almost undifferentiated surface morphology and grain size (Fig. S4) of all PVK films indicates that the interface modification of MBA/CQDs does not damage the PVK films structure. Due to the several nanometers in size of CQDs and their uniformly tight adhesion on the PVK film surface, the QDs are not observed. However, the homogeneous appearance of C, N, O, Cs, Pb and Br elements of the MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 film in the EDS mapping images as shown in Fig. 2 b indicates that MBA/CQDs are uniformly distributed on the CsPbBr3 film, which is further confirmed by the presence of N 1 s characteristic peak for MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 film in Fig. S5. Fig. 2 c is the XRD patterns of CsPbBr3 films with and without modifiers. In addition to the inevitable occurrence of trace CsPb2Br5 phase in all films [40], the diffraction peaks of the (100), (110), (111), (200), (210) and (211) crystal planes of CsPbBr3 PVK also appear and have almost the same intensity, indicating that the MBA/CQDs modification does not change the crystal structure of the PVK itself.

To study the defect state density of the pristine and different CQDs modified PVK films, the hole-only devices with a structure of FTO/NiO/CsPbBr3/without and with different CQDs/Carbon were used for the SCLC characterization, as shown in Fig. 2 d. The trap-filled limit voltage (V TFL) and trap state density (n trap) values of different CsPbBr3 films are summarized in Table S1. Compared with the 6.916 × 1015 cm−3 of n trap for the pristine CsPbBr3 film, it is decreased to 3.409 × 1015 cm−3 for the film modified with 6 MBA/CQDs due to the passivation effect of MBA/CQDs on the uncoordinated ion defects on the surface of CsPbBr3 film. The 6 MBA/CQDs shows the best passivation effect is possibly attributed to the fact that the few MBA cannot sufficiently passivate the defect states and the excessive MBA introduces trap states because of the excess MBA itself as a defect [23]. The reduction in n trap of the modified PVK films will cause an inhibition of carrier recombination, and increases the absorbance of the film modified with MBA/CQDs as the UV–vis absorption spectra shown in Fig. 2 e. This helps to generate more photogenerated carriers to obtain higher current density and PCE outputs.

To investigate the effect of MBA/CQDs modification on the performance of the device, the CsPbBr3 PSCs with a structure of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/(MBA/CQDs)/Carbon were prepared (Fig. 3
a). Fig. 3 b is a cross-sectional SEM image of the actual carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs modification. The device is formed by multiple layers of closely contacted heterostructures, and the MBA/CQDs interlayer is too thin to be observed. The tight and void free contacts in each layer of the device ensure the rapid charge transport and transfer. Moreover, the energy level structure of the CQDs and MBA/CQDs is determined by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves and the Tauc plots, as shown in Fig. S6. As the results described in Fig. 3 c, the MBA/CQDs have a deeper highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level compared with the CQDs because of the fraction increase of sp 2-bonding [41], which is closer to the valence band of CsPbBr3 PVK to effectively reduce the energy barrier of hole transport from CsPbBr3, thereby reducing energy loss in charge transfer and improving charge extraction for higher output of device performance.

The J 1/2-V curves (Fig. 3 d) of the hole-only devices were recorded to extract the hole mobility (μ h) of the CsPbBr3 films with and without different CQDs modification. As listed in Table S2, the μ h of the original film is 2.41 × 10−3 cm2 V−1 s−1, which is significantly improved to 3.32 × 10−3 cm2 V−1 s−1 of the CsPbBr3 film with 6 MBA/CQDs due to the defects passivation of PVK film by MBA/CQDs, the lower edge defects of MBA/CQDs and its more matched energy level with that of PVK. This indicates that the modification of MBA/CQDs accelerates the transport of the photo-generated holes. The shorter carrier lifetime of the MBA/CQDs modified PVK films than that of the pristine and CQDs modified films (Fig. 3 e, Table S3) also verifies the higher hole transport and extraction capacity of MBA/CQDs. The PL fluorescence intensity of the MBA/CQDs modified PVK films is much lower than that of the other films (Fig. 3 f), which cross-demonstrates the fast hole extraction of MBA/CQDs. Moreover, the PL peak of the MBA/CQDs modified PVK films manifests an obvious blue-shift compared with that of the pristine one, also illustrating the effective passivation effect of MBA/CQDs on the surface defects of PVK films, which conforms well with the n trap results (Fig. 2 d) [42].

The J-V curves of various carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs were recorded to intuitively reflect their photovoltaic performance, as shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 3 g. The relevant photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table S4 and Table 1 . After detailed optimization of the concentration of CQDs and their mass ratio to MBA, the champion device with 6 MBA/CQDs acquires a PCE of 10.40 % with a conspicuous V OC of 1.613 V, a short-circuit current density (J SC) of 7.82 mA cm−2 and a fill factor (FF) of 82.46 %, which is a significant improvement over the original device’s PCE of 7.02 % (V OC: 1.431 V, J SC: 6.66 mA cm−2, FF: 73.67 %) and is a cutting-edge value in the CsPbBr3 PSCs efficiencies (Table S5). The decrease in trap states of PVK films and interface energy level mismatch after MBA/CQDs modification and the lower edge defects of MBA/CQDs induced few charge recombination are responsible for the improvement of photovoltaic data for the MBA/CQDs modified device and its lower hysteresis effect (Fig. S8) [43]. As the IPCE spectra shown in Fig. 3 h, the devices with MBA/CQDs show a higher photonic current response between 325 and 530 nm wavelength and a larger integrated J SC value than the original CsPbBr3 PSCs. And the integrated J SC of all devices is basically consistent with that of the J-V curves, confirming the reliability of the performance enhancement for the MBA/CQDs modified devices. The approximate PCE and current density for the steady-state power output curves (Fig. 3 i and Table S6) and the J-V curves, together with the centralized statistical distribution of the devices photovoltaic parameters in Fig. S9, further indicate the high dependability and reproducibility of the device performance.

To clarify the effect of MBA/CQDs modification on the charge extraction and recombination kinetics in the device, series electrochemical characterizations were conducted. The remarkably improved built-in potential (V bi) of the 6 MBA/CQDs integrated device compared to that of the other PSCs in Fig. 4
a illustrates a stronger driving force for separating and extracting photogenerated electrons and holes in the former device [44], because of the improved passivation effect and the higher energy level match of 6 MBA/CQDs toward PVK film. The larger straight-line slope of the MBA/CQDs modified devices than that of the original CsPbBr3 PSCs indicates a lower charge accumulation at the former devices interface, which inhibits charge recombination to achieve higher V OC output. The relationships of V OC and J SC for carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with and without various CQDs modification with the incident light intensity (I) were plotted to study the charge recombination behavior. The factor (n) pertaining to single-molecule recombination can be obtained from the formula: V OC = nkBTln(I)/q + constant [45], as shown in Fig. 4 b, the Boltzmann constant, absolute temperature, and elementary charge are denoted by kB , T and q, respectively. It can be seen that the n value of the 6 MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 PSC is reduced to 1.79 from the 2.10 of the unmodified device, indicating that the modification of 6 MBA/CQDs can effectively suppress single-molecule recombination in the device because of the minimum defects of it modified PVK film. At the same time, the factor (α) related to the bimolecular recombination can be obtained from the J SC-I relationship (Fig. 4 c) by the formula: J SC ∝ Iα
[46]. The larger α values of the MBA/CQDs modified CsPbBr3 PSCs than 0.962 of the unmodified device indicates an inhibited bimolecular recombination in the former PSCs, which is mainly due to the optimization of the interface energy level arrangement after modification of MBA/CQDs and their edge defects reduction.

Generally, the electronic lifetime (τ n) in the device visually reflects the charge recombination and the numerical value can be derived from the V OC decay curves (Fig. S10) according to the formula:
τ n
=
- (k
B
T /
e)(dV OC
/
dt)
- 1
[47], where k B is the Boltzmann constant and T is the absolute temperature. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the PSC modified with 6 MBA/CQDs has the longest τ n value, further validating the significantly reduced carrier recombination in this device. The lower leakage current density of the CsPbBr3 PSCs modified with MBA/CQDs than that of the other devices shown in Fig. 4 e also cross-demonstrates that the recombination in the former PSCs is suppressed. In addition, EIS was measured and fitted with the equivalent circuit to investigate the charge transfer dynamics in the device, as shown in Fig. 4 f. The fitted R s (series resistance) and R rec (recombination resistance) values are presented in Table S7. After modification of MBA/CQDs, the device R s is decreased because of the reduction in edge defects of CQDs with MBA functionalization and the interface connectivity of MBA/CQDs, which is beneficial to promoting the improvement of the FF of the cell. At the same time, the PSCs modified with MBA/CQDs have a larger R rec value, which indicates that the passivation of PVK defects and the optimization of interface energy level arrangement by MBA/CQDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of carriers in the device to enhance J SC and V OC. From the above results, it can be concluded that the modification of MBA/CQDs with lower defects substantially boost charge extraction and restrain charge recombination to significantly improve PSCs photovoltaic performance.

Long-term stability is a crucial factor to be considered when assessing the overall effectiveness of PSCs. Therefore, the stability of the unpackaged carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs with and without 6 MBA/CQDs modification were performed under the ambient environment with 85 °C and 85 % RH, as shown in Fig. 5
a.Compared with the PCE retention rate of 83 % of the original device, the PSC modified with 6 MBA/CQDs maintains 92 % of the initial PCE after aging for 30 days, showing higher humidity and heat tolerance. The decreased defects and the improved hydrophobicity of the CsPbBr3 PVK film with MBA/CQDs modification (as demonstrated by the increased contact angle of PVK/(MBA/CQDs) shown in Fig. 5 b) inhibit the decomposition of PVK crystalline phase under high temperature and humidity conditions, which gives the corresponding device superior stability. The significantly enhanced PCE, long-term wet and thermal stability indicate that the modification of MBA/CQDs is a promising interface engineering to improve the photovoltaic performance of carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs.

In conclusion, the MBA-functionalized CQDs are developed as an effective modifier at PVK/carbon back interface to improve the performance of CsPsBr3 PSCs. By detailed optimization, an encouraging PCE of 10.40 % is achieved for carbon-based CsPsBr3 PSCs with MBA/CQDs, which is remarkably higher than 7.02 % efficiency for the original device. The results show that the dehydration condensation reaction between MBA and CQDs leads to a decrease in edge defects of CQDs, which facilitates charge transport and extraction. Furthermore, the insertion of the MBA/CQDs interface modifier not only holistically passivates the cation and anion defects on the surface of PVK film but also optimizes the band structure between PVK layer and carbon electrode, which improve the hole extraction and reduce the charge recombination and energy loss. The MBA/CQDs modified carbon-based CsPsBr3 PSCs without packaging also exhibit significantly improved humidity and temperature stability after 30 days of aging in ambient air. Our findings provide a new option for designing efficient interface modifier to boost the performance of PSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021ME037), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22179051 and 61604143), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFE0111000), Special Fund of Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqnz20221141), and Foundation of Key Laboratory of Advanced Technique & Preparation for Renewable Energy Materials, Ministry of Education, Yunnan Normal University (OF2022-02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147736.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong carrier lifetime exceeding 20 micros in lead halide perovskite film enable efficient solar cells
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Guo, B. Wang, D. Lu, T. Wang, T. Liu, R. Wang, X. Dong, T. Zhou, N. Zheng, Q. Fu, Z. Xie, X. Wan, G. Xing, Y. Chen, Y. Liu, Ultralong carrier lifetime exceeding 20 micros in lead halide perovskite film enable efficient solar cells, Adv. Mater. 35 (2023) 2212126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface reaction for efficient and stable inverted perovskite solar cells
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Jiang, J. Tong, Y. Xian, R.A. Kerner, S.P. Dunfield, C. Xiao, R.A. Scheidt, D. Kuciauskas, X. Wang, M.P. Hautzinger, R. Tirawat, M.C. Beard, D.P. Fenning, J.J.
